statement.markdown: Given a stack implementation using two lists (push and pop operations), fix the logical bug that causes an infinite loop when popping elements from an empty stack.
statement.inputFormat: Array of integers to push onto the stack, followed by the number of pop operations to simulate.
statement.outputFormat: Resulting stack after the specified pop operations.
statement.notes: Time complexity O(1) per push/pop operation. Space complexity O(n), where n is the number of elements in the stack.

examples.explanation: Push 1 to 5 onto the stack, then pop 4 times to get the resulting stack.

hints.text: Check the pop operation for the empty stack case.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The issue arises when the pop operation is attempted on an empty stack.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would involve checking the stack size before each pop operation, but this can be optimized.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach would be to return a special value or an exception when attempting to pop from an empty stack.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves checking for the empty stack case before attempting to pop an element.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: This solution is correct as it handles the empty stack case properly.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run to understand the solution.
commonMistakes: Infinite loop due to incorrect handling of empty stack

functionSignature.python: def push_and_pop(self, pushed: list[int], M: int) -> list[int]:
